cards.text: {{c1::std::initializer_list}} can be used to implement constructors, assignment operators, and other functions that accept a {{c2::list initialization parameter}}.

cards.question: What does this constructor enable?
cards.explanation: This constructor allows creating an IntArray using brace initialization: IntArray arr{1, 2, 3, 4, 5}; The std::initializer_list parameter accepts the list of values.

cards.text: To enable list initialization for your class, create a constructor that takes {{c1::std::initializer_list<T>}} as a parameter.

cards.front: How do you iterate over elements in a std::initializer_list?
cards.back:
Use a range-based for loop:
for (auto element : list) { /* use element */ }
or use iterators with list.begin() and list.end().

cards.front: How do you get the number of elements in a std::initializer_list?
cards.back:
Use the size() member function: list.size()
Note: size() returns size_t, so you may need to cast it to int if needed.

cards.question: What does this function enable?
cards.explanation: This assignment operator allows assigning a list to an IntArray: arr = {1, 2, 3, 4}; It enables reassigning the array using brace initialization syntax.

cards.text: std::initializer_list is a {{c1::template}} class, so you must specify the {{c2::element type}} it will contain: std::initializer_list<{{c2::int}}>.

cards.front: What syntax does std::initializer_list enable for your custom classes?
cards.back:
It enables brace initialization syntax:
MyClass obj{1, 2, 3}; // construction
obj = {4, 5, 6}; // assignment
This is the same syntax used by std::vector and other STL containers.

cards.front: Which standard library containers support std::initializer_list?
cards.back:
Most STL containers support it, including std::vector, std::array, std::list, std::set, std::map, etc.
Example: std::vector<int> v{1, 2, 3, 4, 5};

cards.explanation:
Provide std::initializer_list constructors for:
  • Container classes that hold multiple elements
  • Classes where list initialization makes semantic sense
  • Classes that need to match STL container initialization syntax
Avoid for:
  • Classes with few members where regular constructors are clearer
  • Classes where the order of initialization matters semantically

cards.back: It returns size_t (an unsigned integer type). You may need to static_cast it to int if you're using it for array indexing or other signed integer contexts.

cards.text: To use std::initializer_list, include the {{c1::<initializer_list>}} header and create a constructor taking {{c2::std::initializer_list<T>}} as a parameter.
std::initializer_list enables initialization syntax like: MyClass obj{{c1::{1, 2, 3}}}; and assignment like: obj {{c2::=}} {{c1::{4, 5, 6}}}.
